advaita.rendering: You cannot see Me in My cosmic form — the one bearing the universal appearance (viśvarūpadhara) — through this very ordinary eye that is your own natural organ of perception (prākṛta svacakṣus). I therefore grant you a divine eye (divya cakṣus); through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — the superlative power of the Lord's yogic sovereignty, an excess of yoga-śakti (yogaśaktyatiśaya).
advaita.doctrinal_note: Śaṅkara frames the divine eye not as devotional grace but as a specific epistemological instrument: the prākṛta eye is structurally incapable of apprehending the viśvarūpa, so Kṛṣṇa supplies a supra-natural cognitive organ to close the gap. The viśvarūpa is still phenomenal display; Brahman as niṣkala remains beyond even this vision.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Śaṅkara: 'dīvyam dadāmi te cakṣuḥ — yenatv śakyase draṣṭum tad dīvyam'; yogam aiśvaram glossed as yogaśaktyatiśaya.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: I shall display the entire cosmos within a single locus of My body; yet you, with your natural eye (prākṛta svacakṣus) — which grasps only bounded and measured objects — cannot see Me in that mode: utterly distinct from everything else (sakala-itara-visajātīya), immeasurable (aparimeya). I grant you a divine, non-natural (aprākṛta) eye, the instrument of My vision; through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — My ananta-jñāna and My ananta-vibhūti in their fullness.
viśiṣṭādvaita.doctrinal_note: Rāmānuja stresses that Bhagavān's form is sakala-itara-visajātīya — categorically unlike all other beings — so no natural faculty can reach it. The divya cakṣus is a positive ontological gift enabling genuine darśana of the Lord's infinite auspicious qualities (kalyāṇa-guṇas) and vibhūtis, not merely an epistemological workaround.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Rāmānuja: 'skaletara-visajātīyam aparimeyam draṣṭum na śakyase'; yogam aiśvaram = 'anantajñānādiyogam anantavibhūtiyogam ca'.

dvaita.rendering: You are incapable of seeing Me — Hari, the supreme independent reality — through your own natural eye, which is a finite instrument belonging to the bound jīva (jīva-svacakṣus). I grant you a divine eye; through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram, the sovereignty that belongs exclusively to Hari and to no other, since the jīva's vision is always dependent on His will.
dvaita.doctrinal_note: Madhva's own commentary on 11.8 is absent from the panel (the bhāṣya source records: 'Sri Madhvacharya did not comment on this śloka'). This rendering is constructed from Madhva's established doctrinal axioms: Hari's svarūpa is utterly inaccessible to the bound jīva; all faculties of perception are dependent (paratantra); the yogam aiśvaram belongs to Hari alone as the independently real (svatantra). Flag: no direct Madhva bhāṣya anchor for this verse.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: ABSENT — Madhva bhāṣya not recorded for this verse; rendering extrapolated from dvaita siddhantas.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Your natural eye — bound by rule (niyamatas) to grasp only limited, measured objects (parimita-grāhin) — cannot see Me in My form of imperishable sovereignty (akṣara-aiśvarya-rūpa). I therefore grant you a divine eye whose nature is non-natural knowledge (aprākṛta-jñāna-ātmaka); through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — the entirety of distinct attributes residing in My own svarūpa (mat-svarūpa-gata), which you must see directly (sākṣāt kuru).
śuddhādvaita.doctrinal_note: Vallabha reads the verse as Kṛṣṇa responding to Arjuna's conditional 'if You think it possible to show' (11.4): the limitation is purely on the receiver's side, not on Kṛṣṇa's. The akṣara-aiśvarya-rūpa is Kṛṣṇa's own intrinsic Puṣṭi nature; the divine eye is a gift of prasāda enabling participation in Kṛṣṇa's own self-perception.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha: 'mām akṣaraiśvaryarūpam draṣṭum na śakyase'; 'aprākṛtajñānātmakadaśanasādhanaṃ cakṣur dadāmi'; 'mat-svarūpa-gatam … sākṣāt kuru'.

bhakti.rendering: With your own fleshly eye (carmacakṣus) — the natural organ of the body — you are not capable, will not be capable, of seeing Me. I therefore grant you a divine, supramundane, knowledge-natured eye (divyam alaukikaṃ jñānātmakaṃ cakṣus). Through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — My singular, extraordinary power to accomplish the impossible (aghaṭita-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya).
bhakti.doctrinal_note: Śrīdhara Svāmī uses carmacakṣus — the skin-eye — to underline the gross materiality of the ordinary faculty. His gloss of yogam aiśvaram as aghaṭita-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya ('capacity to effect that which cannot be effected') is shared with Madhusūdana and signals the miracle-register: the viśvarūpa is not a vision of the absolute but a demonstration of divine practical omnipotence.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Śrīdhara: 'carmacakṣuṣā māṃ draṣṭuṃ na śakyase'; yogam aiśvaram = 'aghaṭitaghaṭanāsāmarthyam'.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Through this natural eye — the eye that is established by one's own inherent nature (svabhāva-siddha) — you are simply not capable of seeing Me in My divine form (divya-rūpa). I grant you a divine (aprākṛta), non-natural eye capable of perceiving My divine form; through it, behold My yogam aiśvaram — the superlative excess (atiśaya) of the capacity to accomplish the impossible (aghaṭana-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya) — which is the extraordinary, uniquely mine (asādhāraṇa) yoga of the Lord.
advaita-bhakti.doctrinal_note: Madhusūdana synthesizes: the eye's incapacity is not merely physical but ontological — svabhāva-siddha means the natural eye is constitutively limited. He pauses on the grammatical question of śakyase / śakṣyase, citing the chāndasa usage of śyan in the daivādi class, preserving scholarly precision even in devotional context. The yogam aiśvaram gloss matches Śrīdhara's but is embedded in Advaita categories.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusūdana: 'svabhāvasiddhena cakṣuṣā māṃ divyarūpaṃ draṣṭum na śakyase'; yogam aiśvaram = 'aghaṭanaghṭanāsāmarthyātiśayam … asādhāraṇam'; grammatical note on śyan-chāndasa preserved.

so_what_questions: Why does Kṛṣṇa assert that the limitation is in Arjuna's faculty — the prākṛta cakṣus — rather than in any withholding on Kṛṣṇa's side? What does this say about the architecture of perception in the Gītā's epistemology?

The divine eye (divya cakṣus) is granted unilaterally, without Arjuna requesting it. What does that structure of unsolicited grace imply about the relationship between yogic vision and earned spiritual achievement?
Five of the six schools gloss yogam aiśvaram as either ananta-vibhūti or aghaṭita-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya ('accomplishing the impossible'). Are these the same claim? What is at stake in each framing?
Śaṅkara's reading treats the viśvarūpa as still a phenomenal event — even the divine eye does not pierce to niṣkala Brahman. If true, what is the viśvarūpa actually showing Arjuna?

The verse sits between Arjuna's conditional request (11.4: 'if You think it possible') and the actual cosmic vision. What work does 11.8 do structurally — why is the granting of the divine eye narrated as a separate moment rather than simply occurring?

Madhva's school holds that all jīva faculties are paratantra (dependent on Hari's will). Does the granting of the divine eye in 11.8 confirm or complicate that doctrine — is it extraordinary grace or merely the normal operation of paratantra?
After the viśvarūpa is withdrawn (11.45–50), Arjuna reverts to ordinary sight. What does the temporary, revocable nature of the divine eye imply about the permanence of transformative vision?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you encounter a situation that seems opaque — a colleague's behavior, a family conflict, a system you cannot parse — notice that the limitation is structural: the prākṛta cakṣus, the habituated eye trained on surfaces, cannot see the underlying unity (ekatā). The practice is not to try harder with the same instrument but to ask: what is the jñāna-eye — the faculty of discernment (viveka) — already showing you that the surface-reading is missing?
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: In every relationship, the person before you is sakala-itara-visajātīya — utterly unlike what your bounded, measuring mind projects onto them. The aprākṛta cakṣus granted by Bhagavān is the capacity for genuine attentiveness, kainkarya-listening: attending to what is actually present in the other (their ananta-vibhūti, their infinite qualification) rather than what the limited eye of past expectation can hold.
everyday_applications.dvaita: You are a dependent being (paratantra jīva); every faculty you exercise — including the capacity to see clearly — is on loan from Hari. When clarity arrives in a decision, in a conversation, in understanding a situation that was previously dark, the Dvaita practice is to attribute it accurately: this was not my insight, it was Hari's disclosure. Gratitude (kṛtajñatā) directed toward the source of vision is itself a form of worship.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Kṛṣṇa's gift of the divine eye is prasāda — grace that precedes merit, that does not wait for you to become worthy. In daily life, the Puṣṭi-mārga analog is: stop trying to earn the insight. Sit with the limitation (prākṛta cakṣus acknowledged honestly), place yourself in the flow of His līlā, and receive what He chooses to show. The akṣara-aiśvarya-rūpa is not a reward; it is what you see when you stop blocking it.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's aghaṭita-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya — the power to accomplish the impossible — is not esoteric. It surfaces in everyday life as the moment when a situation that was knotted becomes simple, when a relationship that seemed broken finds a word that heals it, when a problem you could not solve resolves as if from outside. The Bhakti practice is to recognize these moments as yogam aiśvaram: not coincidence, not your cleverness, but Bhagavān's extraordinary practical sovereignty showing through.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana holds two registers simultaneously: the svabhāva-siddha eye is constitutively limited (Advaita diagnosis), yet Kṛṣṇa's grace is a specific, personal, devotional act (Bhakti response). In daily life, this synthesis means: hold your cognitive limitations honestly — know that your habituated perception is structurally limited — and stay open to the moments when Kṛṣṇa's asādhāraṇa yoga rearranges what you see. Neither self-reliance nor passive waiting: clear-eyed surrender.

self_assessment: Six genuine doctrinal voices rendered with direct bhāṣya anchoring; Madhva absence flagged honestly; aghaṭita-ghaṭanā-sāmarthya convergence across bhakti/advaita-bhakti/śuddhādvaita correctly identified as a shared gloss-cluster worth noting.
